cataloging_subject.label: Chaucerian and Other Pieces
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection from Walter W. Skeat's 1897 Chaucerian and Other Pieces: the printed title page and twenty-one body pieces on pp. 1-145, 191-203, 205-232, 237-296, 299-326, 347-404, and 449-450. It is neither one medieval work nor the complete 1897 volume, and the selected pieces are not works by Geoffrey Chaucer as a group.

catalog_summary.title: Chaucerian and Other Pieces
catalog_summary.author: Thomas Usk; John Gower; Thomas Hoccleve; Henry Scogan; John Lydgate; Sir Richard Roos; John Walton; Sir John Clanvowe (attributed); and anonymous writers
catalog_summary.date: Works composed from about 1387 through the late fifteenth century; edition published 1897
catalog_summary.region: England, with two short selected pieces transmitted in a late Scottish witness in Older Scots form

assertion.notes: The title belongs to Skeat's edition and to the edition-level selection, not to a single medieval composition.

titles.value: Chaucerian and Other Pieces: Edited, from Numerous Manuscripts, Being a Supplement to the Complete Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

scope.description: Expanded title printed on the encoded 1897 title page

assertion.notes: The phrase 'supplement to the Complete Works' describes the publication context and does not assign the selected pieces to Chaucer.

agents.name: Anonymous medieval writers
notes: A cataloging collective for separately anonymous pieces; it does not assert shared authorship.

agents.name: Thomas Usk
notes: Died 1388; authorship of The Testament of Love is supported by the reconstructed acrostic and autobiographical correspondences, but no manuscript witness is known.

agents.name: Thomas Hoccleve
notes: Circa 1367-1426; author and translator-adapter of The Letter of Cupid.

agents.name: Henry Scogan
notes: Died 1407; attribution of A Moral Balade is supported by its early printed and manuscript headings.

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Circa 1370-1449/50; author or attributed author of eight selected poems.

agents.name: Sir Richard Roos
notes: Translator of Alain Chartier's La Belle Dame sans Mercy; one manuscript explicitly names him.

agents.name: Sir John Clanvowe
notes: Died 1391; the most likely author of The Boke of Cupide according to current scholarship. Skeat instead proposed Sir Thomas Clanvowe and an early-fifteenth-century date.

agents.name: John Walton
notes: Translator of Boethius; the selected stanza called Prosperity is extracted from his 1410 prologue.

agents.name: Walter William Skeat
notes: 1835-1912; editor, selector, transcriber, collator, and commentator of the 1897 volume.

scope.description: The 1897 collection and the twenty-one body pieces retained in CME00032
Collation and consultation of printed authorities and manuscripts, varying by piece

assertion.notes: No manuscript survives; the attribution rests principally on Skeat and Bradley's reconstruction of the acrostic and biographical fit.

notes: The poem is an English adaptation and translation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre au dieu d'Amours.

assertion.notes: The six poems have differing kinds of attribution evidence; the collective statement does not erase those local differences.
Skeat argues for Lydgate on stylistic grounds; the manuscript witnesses used do not uniformly name him.
Harley MS 372 explicitly attributes the English translation to Roos.
Findern ends the poem 'Explicit Clanvowe'. Current scholarship strongly prefers Sir John Clanvowe, while Sir Thomas Clanvowe and a scribal attribution remain minority alternatives.
The stanza is lines 83-90 of the prologue to Walton's translation of Boethius rather than an independent Chaucer poem.
Anonymous status does not imply common authorship. Female authorship of The Flower and the Leaf and The Assembly of Ladies remains debated, and common authorship is not established.

date_statements.display: Circa 1387 through the late fifteenth century

assertion.notes: A range summarizing heterogeneous pieces, not a common composition event.
The U-M catalog display says 1894, but the encoded title page and complete-volume catalogs say 1897.
Historical editorial estimate retained as such.
The poem dates Cupid's proclamation to 1402; Hoccleve later copied a revised version in HM 744.
Skeat's circa 1402 date and later proposals extending into the 1420s are not collapsed into one precise date.
Proposals range from about 1441-42 to 1450; Skeat and Furnivall proposed later dates near 1460.
This follows the preferred Sir John Clanvowe attribution and supersedes Skeat's circa 1403 proposal without erasing the historical disagreement.
Date of Walton's source stanza; Arch. Selden B.24 transmits it later in Scottish dress.
This broad cultural-origin statement does not assign one place of composition to all twenty-one pieces.
This records late Scottish transmission and linguistic form in Arch. Selden B.24, not necessarily original composition in Scotland.
Prosperity is an English source stanza transmitted here in late Scottish linguistic dress; Loyalty is likewise printed from Arch. Selden B.24.

scope.description: Skeat's headings, introductions within selected pieces, and textual notes
Rubrics, citations, explicits, and short embedded phrases
French source phrases and comparison material in the edition

form_statements.description: Two prose-centered pieces with embedded verse and nineteen verse pieces

assertion.notes: Chaucerian denotes reception, style, and publication history, not common authorship by Chaucer.

scope.description: The majority of the verse pieces and the prose Testament of Love

subject_statements.label: Women, gender, fidelity, and the querelle des femmes

summaries.value: A discontiguous digital selection from Walter W. Skeat's 1897 supplementary Chaucer volume. It combines Thomas Usk's prose Testament of Love and the anonymous polemic Jack Upland with works by Gower, Hoccleve, Scogan, Lydgate, Roos, Walton, probably Sir John Clanvowe, and several anonymous late-medieval writers. The collection documents how non-Chaucerian works entered and were later removed from the Chaucer canon.

CME00032 preserves the 1897 title page and seven printed-page runs: pp. 1-145, 191-203, 205-232, 237-296, 299-326, 347-404, and 449-450. It omits the volume's introduction and preliminaries, pieces II, VI, XV, XVII, XXII-XXV, the notes on pp. 451-554, glossary, and indices.

extent.description: Twenty-one top-level body pieces. The Testament of Love and Jack Upland are prose-centered but contain eight and four encoded verse lines respectively; the other nineteen pieces are verse. Counts exclude the three verse lines on the title page and include the motto line at the end of Loyalty.

content_parts.label: I. The Testament of Love
content_parts.summary: A three-book prose dialogue and allegory of love, attributed to Thomas Usk and dated about 1387; Skeat reconstructed its disordered third book from Thynne's 1532 print.

content_parts.label: III. Jack Upland
content_parts.summary: An anonymous anticlerical prose interrogation, with a short embedded verse passage, edited from an early printed copy at Gonville and Caius College and compared with Speght.

content_parts.label: IV. The Praise of Peace
content_parts.summary: John Gower's 385-line appeal to Henry IV for peace and good rule, composed in 1399-1400 and collated against the Trentham manuscript.
notes: The XML encodes 404 L elements because headings, Latin matter, or lineation differ from Skeat's stated 385-line work count.

content_parts.label: V. The Letter of Cupid
content_parts.summary: Thomas Hoccleve's 476-line 1402 English adaptation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre au dieu d'Amours, edited primarily from Fairfax 16 with extensive manuscript collation.

content_parts.label: VII. A Moral Balade
content_parts.summary: Henry Scogan's 189-line counsel poem, edited from early prints and collated with Ashmole 59 and Harley 2251.
notes: The XML heading says Thynne 1542, but Skeat's printed errata directs the reader to substitute 1532.

content_parts.label: VIII. The Complaint of the Black Knight
content_parts.summary: John Lydgate's 681-line dream-vision complaint, also called A Complaynte of a Lovers Lyfe; its composition date remains debated.

content_parts.label: IX. The Flour of Curtesye
content_parts.summary: A 270-line Valentine poem by John Lydgate, printed by Skeat from Thynne 1532.

content_parts.label: X. A Balade in Commendation of Our Lady
content_parts.summary: John Lydgate's 140-line Marian ballade, restored from Thynne, Ashmole 59, and the complete copy on the terminal vellum leaves of Sloane 1212.

content_parts.label: XI. To My Soverain Lady
content_parts.summary: A 112-line secular Valentine poem by John Lydgate that earlier printed editions joined to the preceding Marian ballade.

content_parts.label: XII. Ballad of Good Counsel
content_parts.summary: John Lydgate's 133-line counsel poem with the refrain 'A wikked tonge wol alway deme amis', collated with Findern and Harley 2251.

content_parts.label: XIII. Beware of Doubleness
content_parts.summary: A 104-line poem by John Lydgate, based by Skeat on Fairfax 16 and compared with Ashmole 59, Harley 7578, Add MS 16165, and Stow 1561.

content_parts.label: XIV. A Balade Warning Men to Beware of Deceitful Women
content_parts.summary: A 49-line antifeminist ballade attributed by Skeat to Lydgate, based on Trinity R.3.19 and corrected from the more complete Trinity O.9.38 copy.

content_parts.label: XVI. La Belle Dame sans Mercy
content_parts.summary: Sir Richard Roos's 856-line English translation of Alain Chartier's 1424 French poem, probably made circa 1441-1450.

content_parts.label: XVIII. The Cuckoo and the Nightingale; or, The Book of Cupid, God of Love
content_parts.summary: A 290-line bird debate now most often attributed to Sir John Clanvowe and probably composed after circa 1386 but before his death in 1391.

content_parts.label: XIX. Envoy to Alison
content_parts.summary: A 27-line anonymous lyric transmitted after the Cuckoo poem but rejected by Skeat as Clanvowe's work.

content_parts.label: XX. The Flower and the Leaf
content_parts.summary: An anonymous late-fifteenth-century allegorical dream vision, probably circa 1460-1480; the narrator is a woman, but the author's gender is not established.
notes: Skeat counts 595 lines; the XML contains 594 L elements. Speght 1598 is the sole surviving textual authority used by Skeat.

content_parts.label: XXI. The Assembly of Ladies
content_parts.summary: An anonymous 756-line late-fifteenth-century dream vision, probably circa 1470-1480; common authorship with The Flower and the Leaf and female authorship both remain debated.

content_parts.label: XXVI. Prosperity
content_parts.summary: An eight-line stanza from the 1410 prologue to John Walton's translation of Boethius, copied in later Scottish form and misattributed to Chaucer in Arch. Selden B.24.

content_parts.label: XXVII. Leaulte Vault Richesse
content_parts.summary: An anonymous short Scots-form moral poem whose final motto means 'Loyalty is worth riches'; Skeat tentatively places an English original in the later fifteenth century.
notes: The XML's nine L elements comprise eight verse lines plus the concluding motto.

content_parts.label: XXVIII. Sayings Printed by Caxton
content_parts.summary: Three anonymous proverbial verse units totaling fourteen lines, printed with Caxton's Anelida and later reprinted in the 1542 Chaucer edition without an attribution by Caxton to Chaucer.

content_parts.label: XXIX. Balade in Praise of Chaucer
content_parts.summary: An anonymous seven-line praise of Chaucer preserved at the end of the Parliament of Fowls in Trinity R.3.19 and printed by Stow in 1561.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., Chaucerian and Other Pieces (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1897).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. xv-lxxxii; selected texts pp. 1-450
evidence.summary: Complete source edition used for titles, dates, attributions, editorial statements, manuscript authorities, piece boundaries, and the selection-to-volume comparison.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, CME00032.
evidence.summary: Official digital-text landing page and rights assertion; its displayed 1894 publication date conflicts with the encoded 1897 title page.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 79297, Chaucerian and Other Pieces.
evidence.summary: Independent library catalog evidence for Oxford, Clarendon Press, 1897 and the complete extent lxxxiv, 608 pages.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Introduction to The Boke of Cupide, God of Love, or The Cuckoo and the Nightingale.
evidence.summary: Current scholarly discussion of Sir John Clanvowe's likely authorship, pre-1391 dating, witness contents, and the Findern attribution; also identifies Walton's source stanza.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Introduction to A Complaynte of a Lovers Lyfe or The Complaint of the Black Knight.
evidence.summary: Current attribution and dating discussion plus a detailed witness census and defect notes.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Introduction to La Belle Dame sans Mercy.
evidence.summary: Current discussion of Roos's attribution, translation date, manuscript groups, disordered copies, and relationship to Chartier's 1424 poem.

evidence.citation: Derek Pearsall, ed., The Floure and the Leafe, The Assembly of Ladies, The Isle of Ladies, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.summary: Treats both works as anonymous late-fifteenth-century dream visions and explains why common and female authorship remain hypotheses rather than established facts.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Cambridge University Library MS Ff.1.6 (Findern).
evidence.summary: Provides old-foliation contents and warns that the CUDL digitization uses a new foliation omitting missing leaves and stubs; verified against the CUDL facsimile.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for British Library Add MS 59495 (Trentham manuscript).
evidence.summary: Official witness identity, date, extent, and Praise of Peace folios are documented in the lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Huntington Library HM 744.
evidence.summary: Official catalog and facsimile evidence for Hoccleve's autograph Letter of Cupid at ff. 39v-50v.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for British Library Harley MS 2251.
evidence.summary: Official catalog evidence for Scogan's Moral Balade and the warning poem's witness folios.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for British Library Add MS 16165, Add MS 34360, Harley MS 7578, and related literary manuscripts.
evidence.summary: Official catalog records establish current shelfmarks, contents, dates, folios, and unavailable legacy digital-image links.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for British Library Harley MS 372.
evidence.summary: Official catalog identifies Roos and locates La Belle Dame sans Mercy at ff. 61r-69v.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Trinity College Cambridge MSS R.3.19, R.3.20, and O.9.38.
evidence.summary: Official catalogs and IIIF manifests establish the witnesses, relevant folios or pages, complete-object boundaries, and CC BY-NC 4.0 terms.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Bodleian Library MS Arch. Selden B.24.
evidence.summary: Official catalog and full IIIF facsimile establish the Scottish witness, physical sequence, and relevant folios.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Bodleian Library MS Fairfax 16.
evidence.summary: Official catalog and full IIIF facsimile establish relevant work folios and manuscript contents.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for British Library Sloane MS 1212.
evidence.summary: Catalog and Skeat evidence identify the complete Commendation of Our Lady on the terminal vellum leaves of a Hoccleve manuscript.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for British Library Add MS 34360.
evidence.summary: Official catalog identifies The Assembly of Ladies at ff. 37r-49r and records the manuscript's date and unavailable legacy images.

open_questions.question: Can the authorship, common authorship, or authorial gender of The Flower and the Leaf and The Assembly of Ladies be established more securely?
next_steps: Retain anonymous responsibility unless new manuscript or attribution evidence emerges.
Review current scholarship without treating a female narrator as proof of authorial gender.

open_questions.question: Does the Findern 'Explicit Clanvowe' name Sir John Clanvowe, Thomas Clanvowe, or a copyist?
next_steps: Prefer Sir John Clanvowe as the current best-supported attribution while preserving alternatives.
Reassess if new paleographical or metrical evidence appears.

open_questions.question: Why does the U-M display record assign 1894 to an item whose encoded title page and complete-volume catalogs say 1897?
next_steps: Treat 1894 as a catalog error or series-level date unless U-M supplies contrary evidence.
Keep the direct title-page date 1897 as the edition publication date.

notes: The U-M inherited author display 'Chaucer, Geoffrey' is not used as authorship evidence for the edited selection.
Piece numbers retain gaps because CME00032 omits several pieces from Skeat's complete volume.
Exact source witnesses, facsimile boundaries, access routes, rights layers, and local source-cache files are recorded in the paired lineage manifest.
